pair_convention.spot_explanation: AUD/USD at 0.7247 means 1 Australian Dollar buys 0.7247 US Dollars. A rising AUD/USD rate means the Australian Dollar is strengthening against the US Dollar (each AUD buys more USD). A falling rate means AUD is weakening versus USD. The pair is quoted as USD per AUD, so bullish AUD/USD positions profit when the AUD appreciates relative to the USD.
pair_convention.base_dossier_tactical_stance: Neutral with mild Bullish tilt (weighted score +0.253)

executive_summary: AUD/USD trades at 0.7247 (latest close), having hit a fresh four-year high of 0.7280 on 6 May before consolidating. The pair has rallied 1.09% over the past month, 2.32% over three months, and a commanding 12.60% over one year, placing spot at the 98th percentile of its 52-week range. The dominant bilateral narrative is AUD outperformance driven by peak RBA-Fed rate divergence (+73bp policy gap, widest in the cycle) colliding with structural USD weakness from stagflation, Fed institutional fracture, and dollar-smile-bottom dynamics. Both standalone dossiers reinforce the pair direction — the AUD dossier is tactically mildly bullish AUD while the USD dossier is tactically mildly bearish USD — creating an amplified bullish AUD/USD signal that is modestly stronger than either leg alone. The key bilateral tension is between this macro tailwind and severely crowded AUD speculative positioning (CFTC combined +93k contracts, 85-90th percentile), the unexpected March trade deficit (first since 2017), and the asymmetric Hormuz-reopening scenario that would simultaneously weaken AUD (commodity premium removed) and modestly support USD (tail-risk flows). Net stance: tactically Mildly Bullish AUD/USD with Conviction 3/5 at 62% confidence; strategically Mildly Bullish with Conviction 3/5 at 58% confidence, though the strategic path involves a mid-year pullback toward 0.70–0.71 before resumption higher as Fed easing deepens.

market_context.valuation_assessment: AUD is approximately 8.6% above PPP fair value versus USD (implied fair value ~0.66 per the AUD dossier). The BIS REER for AUD is estimated around 115–116, in the richest territory since 2017. The USD dossier simultaneously shows USD ~7% overvalued versus AUD on PPP (implied fair value AUD/USD 0.71). The combined PPP signal suggests AUD/USD spot at 0.7247 is modestly above the bilateral midpoint of fair value estimates (0.66–0.71 range), placing the pair in the upper quartile of its cyclical valuation band. This richness is structural — AUD has traded above PPP for most of the post-2015 period — but the magnitude argues against chasing further upside at current levels.

positioning.quote_cftc_net: Aggregate G10 short-USD positioning most extreme in over a decade; DXY net-spec at 18th percentile after rebuilding from 14-year bear extreme
positioning.net_pair_implication: Both legs are positioned for AUD/USD to rise — AUD longs stretched at the 85–90th percentile AND USD shorts crowded at decade extremes — creating an amplified double-crowding signal that argues for violent downside if any catalyst triggers simultaneous unwind of AUD longs and USD shorts.
positioning.crowding_assessment: Pair trade crowded on long side — bilateral double-extreme positioning at historically rare levels

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA: 0.7071, 100d SMA: 0.7061, 200d SMA: 0.6841. Configuration is bullishly stacked: spot (0.7247) > 50d (0.7071) > 100d (0.7061) > 200d (0.6841), supporting trend-following CTA long positioning.
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Consolidation near resistance following spike to 0.7280 on 6 May. The rally pushed into the May 2022 swing area (0.7265–0.7283) where sellers leaned against historical resistance. Near-term pullback toward 0.7193–0.7200 swing support with H4 100-period EMA at 0.7158 as a deeper pullback target.

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV-IV gap is small, suggesting no significant variance risk premium. Realised vol slightly below implied, consistent with a low-VRP environment.
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: 1M 25-delta risk reversals have moved less negative through Q1 2026 as RBA hawkishness was priced, with calls bid relative to puts — reflecting reduced demand for AUD/USD downside protection relative to upside.
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Mild backwardation around the June RBA and FOMC cluster, consistent with modest event premium pricing for the binary data cluster of 12–17 June.
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Low vol / compressing — IV at 35–45th percentile, below median, options cheap relative to positioning extremes

market_context.recent_regime: The pair has been in a steady uptrend since the April 2025 trough at 0.5915, rallying over 22% to the 6 May high of 0.7280 — a new four-year peak. The most recent price action shows a consolidation pattern between 0.7100 and 0.7280 following the post-RBA spike on 5–6 May. The narrative has shifted from Iran-war-driven risk-off (which briefly crushed AUD to 0.59 in April 2025) to a combination of RBA hawkish carry, commodity premium, and structural USD weakness. The post-RBA consolidation at elevated levels suggests distribution risk as momentum wanes at multi-year resistance.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move only ~8 pips against before carry is erased over a 28-day tactical horizon (73bp ÷ 252 × 28 ≈ 8 pips; 0.7247 → 0.7239)

forward_points.1m: -3 to -5 pips (AUD at premium, forward reflects AUD rate advantage)
forward_points.6m: -25 to -35 pips (6M forward outright estimated near 0.7210–0.7220)

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The RBA-Fed rate differential of +73bp annualised is positive but marginal for AUD/USD specifically. The carry-to-vol ratio of 0.09 is well below the 0.20 attractiveness threshold typically cited for carry trades, meaning AUD/USD is not a compelling carry vehicle in isolation. The breakeven move of just 8 pips provides minimal cushion against spot volatility averaging 50–80 pips per day. The same RBA advantage is materially better expressed in AUD/JPY (carry-to-vol ~0.38) or AUD/NZD (carry-to-vol ~0.32), where the rate differential is larger and/or pair volatility is lower. The true bilateral edge in AUD/USD lies in the rate-path divergence — the RBA approaching terminal while the Fed is transitioning to cuts — rather than the current nominal carry quantum. Forward points are negative (AUD at small forward discount to USD) but trivially small relative to the pair's daily move.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The RBA-Fed policy rate differential of +73bp (RBA 4.35% vs Fed 3.625%) is at its widest point in the cycle and represents the clearest bilateral driver of AUD/USD strength. The RBA has delivered three consecutive 25bp hikes with an 8-1 vote, while the Fed held with an extraordinary 8-4 dissent — the most fractured FOMC since October 1992. The AU-US 10Y sovereign spread at +62bp (ACGB 4.99% vs UST 4.35%) is at a multi-year high and supports portfolio-rebalancing flows into Australian bonds. OIS markets price a ~20% probability of another RBA hike in June and ~68% by August, whereas the Fed is priced for no action until a possible 25bp cut in September. The bilateral carry of +73bp annualised is positive but modest; the true bilateral edge lies in the rate-path divergence — the RBA is still hiking or at peak while the Fed is transitioning to cuts under Warsh, creating a widening expected differential. Markets are pricing around just a 20% chance of another rate hike in June by the Reserve Bank, after 75 bps of tightening to 4.35% over the past three meetings, with the probability rising to about 68% for August, with a terminal rate near 4.6% almost fully priced in by September.
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA Cash Rate: 4.35% vs Fed Funds midpoint: 3.625% = +73bp AUD advantage
AU-US 10Y spread: +62bp (multi-year high)
OIS-implied June RBA: ~20% hike probability; Fed June: ~80% hold, ~20% cut
Bilateral carry: +73bp annualised; carry-to-vol 0.09 (thin)
AU 2Y: 4.10% vs US 2Y: 3.85% = +25bp front-end differential
tactical.what_is_priced: The current spot level (~0.7247) reflects the full peak-carry premium. Markets price one RBA hike by September and one Fed cut — any deviation in either direction would move the pair. The mispricing lies in the speed of Fed easing under Warsh: if the Fed delivers 50bp+ by year-end versus 25bp priced, the bilateral differential widens further.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic rates outlook remains AUD-positive but with diminishing conviction. The RBA is approaching its terminal rate (consensus 4.35–4.85%), and the May SMP projects the first cut for early 2027. Meanwhile, the Fed under Warsh is expected to deliver 50–75bp of cuts through H2 2026. This means the AU-US differential should at minimum hold at current levels and potentially widen by 25–50bp over the strategic window. The bilateral carry channel remains supportive even as AUD approaches terminal, because the Fed is moving in the opposite direction. However, the RBA's own projection of trimmed mean returning to 3.1% by Q2 2027 implies the cutting cycle will eventually begin, capping the duration of AUD carry advantage. The 10Y spread trajectory should remain AUD-favourable as UST long-end yields face supply pressure from the $79bn Q2 borrowing revision and war supplemental costs.
key_bilateral_metrics: Expected policy gap 6M forward: +100 to +135bp (RBA 4.35–4.60% vs Fed 3.25–3.50%)
AU-US 10Y spread 6M projection: +50 to +75bp (from current +62bp)
First RBA cut: Q1–Q2 2027 (priced); first Fed cut: Q3 2026 (priced)
Carry-to-vol 6M: improving as differential widens and vol stays subdued
Westpac terminal RBA forecast: 4.85% (most hawkish)
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets discount the RBA approaching terminal and the Fed delivering modest cuts. The unpriced element is the magnitude of Fed easing — a dovish Warsh-led FOMC delivering 75bp+ by year-end would widen the bilateral gap beyond current market expectations, supporting AUD/USD above 0.72.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical and strategic stances are aligned (both Bullish), with tactical confidence higher (72 vs 60) because the near-term rate differential is already realised and working, while the strategic view depends on the uncertain pace of Fed easing and RBA terminal positioning.

carry_analysis.summary: The +73bp RBA-Fed carry is positive but below the 0.20 carry-to-vol attractiveness threshold. Better expressed in AUD/JPY (0.38) or AUD/NZD (0.32). The true bilateral edge is the rate-path divergence — RBA at/near peak vs Fed transitioning to cuts — rather than current carry quantum.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral inflation picture strongly favours AUD/USD upside. Australian headline CPI at 4.6% YoY has triggered an active hawkish RBA response (three consecutive hikes), while US headline CPI at 3.3% with core PCE at 3.2% has produced a paralysed Fed (8-4 split, no move). The critical distinction is the central bank reaction function: Australia's inflation problem is being met with decisive tightening, while US stagflation (Q1 GDP +2.0% miss, GDP price index +4.5%) is producing policy inertia. Growth differentials are modestly AUD-positive: Australian GDP at +2.1% YoY (with Westpac nowcasting Q4 at +1.1% QoQ) versus US Q1 at +2.0% SAAR with the Q2 outlook contested (Atlanta Fed GDPNow +3.7% but likely overstated). Michigan 1Y inflation expectations spiking to 4.7% (the largest one-month jump since April 2025) further undermines USD as it signals potential inflation de-anchoring.
key_bilateral_metrics: AU headline CPI 4.6% vs US headline CPI 3.3% = +130bp inflation differential
AU trimmed mean 3.3% vs US core PCE 3.2% = marginal difference
AU GDP +2.1% YoY vs US GDP +2.0% SAAR = broadly comparable
AU unemployment 4.3% vs US unemployment 4.3% = identical
Michigan 1Y inflation expectations: 4.7% (+90bp MoM spike)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price inflation in both economies as elevated but manageable. The unpriced element is the data release cluster: Q1 WPI (14 May) and April Labour Force (15 May) for Australia, plus April CPI (12 May) for the US, create binary risk within the tactical window. A firm AU WPI (3.6%) combined with a cool US CPI (<3.2%) would amplify the AUD/USD bullish case significantly.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the inflation/growth outlook becomes more balanced. Both economies face the disinflation test of Q2 2026 — if Brent normalises toward $80–90, both headline inflation prints should moderate, reducing the hawkish impulse for the RBA while simultaneously allowing the Fed to cut. The RBA's SMP forecasts trimmed mean peaking at 3.8% in Q2 before falling to 3.1% by Q2 2027; the Fed's implied path sees core PCE at 2.5–3.0% by year-end. The net bilateral effect is roughly neutral because both central banks would be on converging paths — the RBA easing into 2027, the Fed easing into H2 2026. Growth convergence also reduces the differential: Australian growth may soften as the cumulative 4.35% cash rate transmits through mortgage refinancing, while US growth faces tariff-related drag.
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA forecast trimmed mean Q2 2027: 3.1% vs Fed-implied core PCE year-end: 2.5–3.0%
RBA forecast unemployment Q2 2028: 4.7% vs US consensus year-end: 4.5%
Severe oil scenario (35% probability) extends inflation in both economies
Q2 trimmed mean print (late July) is the key AUD binary
Q2 US GDP (July) is the key USD binary
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price parallel disinflation, which is roughly our base case. The bilateral edge is in the sequencing: Australia peaks later (Q2 2026) than the US (Q1 2026), meaning the RBA hawkish premium persists slightly longer than the market discounts.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bullish reflects the active RBA hawkish response versus Fed paralysis; strategic Neutral reflects the convergence of both economies toward similar inflation trajectories over 6 months.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The risk-on environment with S&P 500 at record highs (7,365), VIX at 17.4, and IG OAS near 25-year tights is strongly AUD/USD-positive. AUD is a high-beta risk-on currency while USD sits at the bottom of the dollar smile in this goldilocks regime. AUD/USD has historically rallied during risk-on episodes as capital flows from USD reserves into higher-yielding commodity currencies. The Iran ceasefire narrative (Rubio declaring Operation Epic Fury 'over') has catalysed the latest leg of risk-on enthusiasm, directly benefiting AUD/USD through both the AUD leg (risk-on proxy) and USD leg (safe-haven unwind). However, AUD's correlation to risk has decoupled from historical norms (rolling 60d beta to S&P around 0.30 vs typical 0.40–0.50), with carry and commodity channels now dominant. This decoupling means AUD/USD may underperform a continued equity rally but is also partially insulated from a modest equity pullback.
key_bilateral_metrics: VIX: 17.4 (30th percentile); S&P 500: 7,365 (record)
AUD/USD beta to S&P 500 (60d rolling): ~0.30 (below normal 0.40–0.50)
Dollar smile position: USD at bottom (weak in goldilocks)
Iron ore: $105–108/t (stable, AUD-supportive)
IG OAS: ~80bp (25-year tights)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price benign risk continuation. AUD/USD upside from further risk-on is limited given decoupled correlation, but downside from risk-off is meaningful due to AUD's high-beta character. The pair is asymmetrically exposed to risk-off shocks.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the risk regime is more uncertain. A 30% recession probability (Goldman/JPM) and the BofA Bull/Bear at 9.4 (hyper-bull, historical sell signal) create meaningful tail risk for a risk-off episode that would temporarily lift USD and crush AUD. AUD/USD typically suffers 5–10% drawdowns during risk-off events. The 6-month window contains Q2 earnings season (mid-July), August low-volume seasonality, and US midterms (November) — all potential volatility catalysts. Net: the distribution of risk-regime outcomes over 6 months is wide enough to warrant a neutral stance.
key_bilateral_metrics: Recession probability: JPM 35%, Goldman 30%
BofA Bull/Bear: 9.4 (hyper-bull, sell signal)
Historical AUD/USD drawdown in risk-off: 5–10%
Q2 earnings season: mid-July stress test
August seasonality: historically weak for AUD
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price goldilocks continuation at record equity levels. A risk-off reversal is underpriced, and AUD/USD is asymmetrically exposed to the downside in that scenario.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bullish reflects the current dominant risk-on regime supporting AUD/USD; strategic Neutral reflects the wider distribution of risk-regime outcomes over 6 months.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: +0.03 tactical / +0.02 strategic: bilateral double-crowding at extremes warrants higher weight than default

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Positioning is the single most bearish factor for AUD/USD. On the AUD leg, combined CFTC spec/AM net longs at +93k contracts (33.5% of OI, 85–90th percentile) represent the most stretched bullish AUD positioning in over a year. On the USD leg, aggregate G10 short-USD positioning is the most extreme in over a decade. For AUD/USD specifically, both legs are positioned for the pair to rise — this double-crowding amplifies unwind risk. Historical analogues: May 2017 saw AUD specs at +84k contracts preceding an 11% decline; January 2018 at +75k preceded a 14% decline. Any negative catalyst — Hormuz reopening, soft AU employment, hot US CPI — would trigger simultaneous unwinding of AUD longs and USD shorts, generating an outsized AUD/USD selloff of potentially 2–3% within days.
key_bilateral_metrics: AUD combined CFTC longs: +92,952 (5Y percentile 85–90th)
AUD asset managers WoW: +8,589 (still accumulating)
USD aggregate G10 short positioning: most extreme in 10 years
AUD/USD bilateral crowding: double-extreme (both legs positioned for upside)
Estimated unwind magnitude: 2–3% AUD/USD selloff on trigger
tactical.what_is_priced: Risk reversals reportedly less negative (calls bid vs puts) — suggesting downside hedges are cheap relative to the asymmetric positioning. The market is not adequately pricing the double-crowding unwind risk.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, AUD positioning needs to normalise from +93k toward the 5Y mean of +20–30k — a process that mechanically removes AUD bid. The structural narrative supporting long AUD (highest G10 carry, commodity premium) needs to remain intact for new longs to replace exiting ones. As the narrative weakens over 1–6 months (RBA approaches terminal, Hormuz normalises, carry advantage diminishes), positioning normalisation should accelerate. On the USD side, structural short-USD positioning has support from rate convergence and fiscal concerns and is unlikely to flip long, limiting the USD-leg unwind contribution. The net 6-month positioning signal is AUD/USD bearish but at lower conviction than tactical because the positioning unwind is a slower process over quarters.
key_bilateral_metrics: AUD positioning mean-reversion target: +20–30k contracts (from +93k)
Mechanical AUD downside from full normalisation: 5–8%
USD aggregate short positioning: unlikely to flip to net long
Asset manager flow lag: 3–6 months typically
Combined bilateral unwind magnitude: 3–5% AUD/USD downside
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price gradual positioning normalisation; strategic options do not adequately price the tail risk of a clustered unwind (multiple catalysts hitting simultaneously).

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bearish, with tactical confidence higher (72 vs 58) reflecting the immediacy of potential unwind catalysts.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: Both AUD longs (+93k, 85–90th percentile) and USD shorts (most extreme G10 short in a decade) are simultaneously at extremes — a double-crowding signal that is historically rare and argues for 2–4% AUD/USD downside on any negative catalyst
tactical.unwind_trigger: Hormuz definitive reopening (Brent <$90), soft AU April employment print (unemployment 4.5%), or hot US CPI (≥3.5%) triggering simultaneous AUD long liquidation and USD short covering
tactical.historical_analogue: May 2017: AUD specs +84k → 11% AUD/USD decline; January 2018: AUD specs +75k → 14% decline. Combined USD-short crowding analogue: January 2018 dollar squeeze saw simultaneous AUD long / USD short unwind.
tactical.contrarian_case: Positioning at the 88th percentile with +93k AUD combined longs, combined with the most extreme G10 short-USD positioning in a decade, creates a textbook contrarian-bearish signal for AUD/USD. The double-crowding is historically rare — both the AUD long and USD short are at or near extreme levels simultaneously. Historical analogues suggest 2–4% AUD/USD downside when either leg alone is at this extreme; the combined signal is potentially more severe.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: Positioning normalisation from +93k toward the 5Y mean of +20–30k is a multi-quarter headwind for AUD/USD; combined with a mature 12-month rally (+12.6%) and rich REER (~115), positioning dynamics argue for a distribution phase through Q3 2026
strategic.unwind_trigger: Hormuz fully reopens (Brent <$80), China property deterioration drives iron ore below $85, or RBA delivers a dovish pivot — any of which removes the structural narrative supporting AUD longs
strategic.historical_analogue: Post-2017 AUD positioning normalisation: after +84k peak, specs normalised to near-zero over 6–9 months, driving AUD/USD from 0.81 to 0.69. Post-2018 USD squeeze: short-USD normalization contributed to a 5% DXY rally in Q2 2018.
strategic.contrarian_case: At strategic horizons, positioning normalisation from extreme levels combined with a mature trend (12-month rally +12.6%) and rich valuation (REER ~115) historically marks distribution phases. The strategic contrarian view targets AUD/USD at 0.70–0.71 by Q3–Q4 2026 before any structural resumption, with full mean-reversion toward 0.66–0.68 remaining a tail risk if the structural narrative breaks down.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral external balance picture has shifted notably with the March 2026 trade data. In March 2026, Australia posted its first trade deficit since December 2017, with ABS data showing a AUD 1,841 million shortfall. Exports fell 2.7% while imports jumped 14.1%, led by a massive 204.4% rise in ADP equipment. This was driven by a combination of higher costs for fuel imports, continued volatility in gold trade, and most surprisingly, a truly remarkable surge in imports for data processing machines associated with AI. The US current account deficit at -3.0% of GDP is structurally worse than Australia's -2.9%, but Australia's deterioration is accelerating while the US position is stable. Net commodity exposure for this pair: oil rising is mildly AUD-positive (via LNG) and mildly USD-positive (via net oil exporter status) — largely offsetting. Iron ore at $105–108 is stable and modestly AUD-supportive. The tactical terms-of-trade picture is roughly neutral because the March trade surprise undermines the AUD ToT story while elevated energy prices still support it through the LNG channel.
key_bilateral_metrics: AU March trade deficit: -AUD 1.84bn (first since Dec 2017)
AU Q4 2025 current account: -2.9% of GDP vs US: -3.0% of GDP
Iron ore: $105–108/t (stable, AUD-supportive)
Brent: $101–113 range (net effect roughly neutral for AUD/USD)
AU import surge: +14.1% MoM (data centre equipment driven)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price stable AU terms of trade. The March trade deficit surprise is fresh news and likely not fully digested — if April data confirms the trend, AUD faces further external-balance headwinds.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At strategic horizons, the bilateral external balance turns more balanced. The US twin deficit (fiscal -5.8% + CA -3.0% = -8.8%) is structurally worse than Australia's position and argues for strategic USD weakness. However, Australia's current account is deteriorating (Q4 2025 deficit AUD 21.1bn, widest since 2015) while the data-centre import boom could persist, keeping imports elevated. The commodity terms-of-trade story depends on Iran resolution: base case (40%) sees Brent reverting to $80–90 and iron ore stable at $90–100, which reduces the energy-driven AUD premium but doesn't collapse it. The bilateral netting: oil normalisation is mildly AUD-negative (removes LNG premium) and marginally USD-negative (removes petrodollar support). Iron ore stable is AUD-neutral. The US twin deficit structural drag is USD-negative over the strategic horizon.
key_bilateral_metrics: US twin deficit: -8.8% of GDP (worst since 1985 Plaza era)
AU current account trajectory: deteriorating (-2.9% of GDP)
Brent 6M base case: $80–90 (assumes partial Hormuz normalisation)
Iron ore 6M base case: $90–100
China share of AU exports: 30–35%
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus forecasts assume gradual ToT moderation. The bilateral edge is in the US twin-deficit structural channel, which should dominate over 6 months as Treasury supply accelerates.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral, reflecting roughly balanced bilateral external dynamics. Tactical neutrality driven by the unexpected March trade deficit offsetting commodity support; strategic neutrality reflects US twin-deficit drag being offset by AU CA deterioration.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: AUD is a commodity-linked currency with key exposures to iron ore (~$105–108/t, 30–35% of exports to China), LNG/energy (benefiting from elevated Brent $101–113), and gold. Commodity exports are the primary driver of Australia's terms of trade.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: USD has indirect commodity linkages. The US is a net oil exporter, meaning higher oil prices are marginally USD-positive via petrodollar flows and energy sector revenues, but also USD-negative via inflation pass-through. USD is the global commodity pricing currency — extreme commodity moves create safe-haven USD demand.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil up — AUD-positive via LNG/energy export revenues (+); USD-positive via petrodollar flows (+). Net for AUD/USD: roughly offsetting, slight AUD edge because Australia's LNG revenue sensitivity is direct while US petrodollar benefit is indirect. Step 2: Iron ore up — AUD-positive (direct export revenue); USD-neutral. Net for AUD/USD: clearly AUD-positive. Step 3: Oil down (Hormuz reopening) — AUD-negative (removes energy inflation premium that supported RBA hawkishness); USD-negative (removes petrodollar) but USD also benefits from risk-on safe-haven unwind. Net for AUD/USD: mildly AUD-negative because the commodity premium removal hits AUD more directly. Step 4: Combining: the dominant commodity driver for AUD/USD is iron ore (cleaner AUD sensitivity) followed by oil (ambiguous net effect). The March trade data confirms growing import sensitivity (data centre equipment) which offsets commodity export gains.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Brent +10% (~$111 → $122): Mildly AUD/USD-positive via LNG premium and RBA inflation hawkishness extension; partially offset by global risk-off and USD safe-haven bid. Net: AUD/USD +0.5 to +1.0%.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Brent -10% (~$111 → $100): Mildly AUD/USD-negative via LNG premium removal and reduced RBA hawkish impetus; partially offset by risk-on bid reducing USD. Net: AUD/USD -0.5 to -1.5% (asymmetrically more negative because the AUD commodity premium unwind is faster than USD safe-haven unwind).
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Iron ore is the dominant commodity driver for AUD/USD specifically — it provides a pure AUD sensitivity with no offsetting USD effect, unlike oil which affects both legs. Iron ore sustaining $110+ would be a clear AUD/USD positive; iron ore falling below $90 would be a clear AUD/USD negative.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral credibility differential strongly favours AUD/USD. The RBA under Bullock has built credibility through three consecutive hikes — decisive, transparent, data-dependent communication that markets trust. In contrast, the Fed faces an institutional credibility crisis: the 8-4 dissent (highest since 1992), the Powell-Warsh transition creating dual-leadership ambiguity, and ongoing DOJ subpoena litigation threatening Fed independence. Bullock's communication — 'well placed to respond' and 'sit and see' — is clear forward guidance that the market can price. Powell's 'move in either direction' framing is deliberately ambiguous and creates uncertainty premium. The bilateral guidance divergence is AUD-positive: the RBA offers clarity (pause with hawkish optionality) while the Fed offers confusion (transition, dissent, political pressure).
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA vote: 8-1 (clear majority) vs FOMC vote: 8-4 (most fractured since 1992)
RBA credibility: enhanced by 3 consecutive hikes delivering on warnings
Fed credibility: eroded by institutional fracture and transition
Next RBA: 16 June (clear pause expected) vs FOMC: 17 June (Warsh's first, uncertain)
Bullock track record: hawkish pivots in Feb/March/May 2026
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price RBA guidance as fairly aligned with stated path. The Fed credibility erosion is partially priced via the 2.6% YTD DXY decline. The unpriced element is the Warsh transition surprise potential — any hawkish Warsh communication would narrow this bilateral gap.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the bilateral credibility channel remains AUD-positive. The RBA's track record of consistent delivery builds a credibility premium that supports AUD over the strategic horizon. Warsh-led Fed faces a series of credibility tests: the 16-17 June FOMC (his first with new SEP), Jackson Hole in August, and navigating political pressure from Trump's record-low approval ratings. The structural communication gap — RBA provides 8 meetings + 4 SMPs with explicit forecasts, while the Fed faces dual-leadership and partisan tensions — means the RBA offers higher information content per event. This is AUD-positive because transparency reduces risk premium.
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA meetings remaining in 2026: 4 (June, August, September, November)
FOMC meetings remaining in 2026: 5 (June, July, September, November, December)
Jackson Hole (August): Warsh's first framework speech
US midterms (November): political risk for Fed independence
Probability of rating outlook change 6M: AU <5%, US ~10%
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price intact RBA credibility and questioned Fed credibility. The strategic edge is small but persistent as credibility differentials accumulate over quarters.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Bullish, with strategic confidence marginally higher (62 vs 60) as the credibility gap compounds over time.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The Iran-US war wind-down creates asymmetric event risk for AUD/USD. The upside scenario (continued disruption keeping AUD bid via commodity premium) is largely priced, while the downside scenario (definitive Hormuz reopening triggering oil collapse and AUD long unwind) is not fully priced. The 12 May Australian Federal Budget is a separate but lower-volatility event. The 14 May WPI and 15 May Labour Force create binary domestic risk. The Powell-Warsh transition on 15 May creates USD-specific uncertainty. Net bilateral event risk is roughly balanced: the major AUD-bearish catalyst (Hormuz reopening) is offset by the major USD-bearish catalyst (dovish Warsh confirmation). The currency is on track for a roughly 0.1% weekly gain, as fresh clashes between the US and Iran weakened hopes for a near-term peace agreement, boosting demand for the safe-haven US dollar.
key_bilateral_metrics: Hormuz reopening probability (4 weeks): ~25–30%
Federal Budget: 12 May (modest AUD impact expected)
WPI: 14 May (AUD binary catalyst)
Labour Force: 15 May (AUD binary catalyst)
US CPI: 12 May (USD binary catalyst)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price ~75% probability of sustained ceasefire. The binary catalyst cluster (12–15 May) creates elevated near-term event risk that is roughly balanced for AUD/USD direction.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategic event risk is balanced across multiple scenarios. Iran ceasefire stability (40% lasting), China policy risk (Politburo July), US midterms (November), and tariff uncertainty create a wide distribution of outcomes. The bilateral event sensitivity is higher for Australia than the US due to AUD's commodity and China linkages, but the US faces unique political risks (Fed independence, midterms, tariff chaos). Net: roughly balanced.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran ceasefire 6M probability: 60% lasting
China Politburo: July 2026
US midterms: 3 November 2026
Tariff escalation risk: 25%
AU federal election: May 2028 (no near-term risk)
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price benign resolution of most event risks; tail risks are underpriced on both sides of the pair.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Neutral, reflecting balanced bilateral event risks.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Australia's fiscal position (AAA/Stable from S&P, Aaa from Moody's, gross debt ~33% of GDP) is materially stronger than the US (AA+ from S&P, Aa1 from Moody's, debt-to-GDP 101%). The differential is structural but not normally a tactical driver. The 12 May Australian Federal Budget is the near-term focus but typically produces modest AUD moves. The US fiscal deterioration (CBO projects -5.8% of GDP deficit, 30Y briefly 5%) is a slow-burning USD-negative that rarely moves the pair over 4 weeks.
key_bilateral_metrics: AU rating: AAA/Stable vs US rating: AA+/Aa1
AU debt-to-GDP: ~33% vs US debt-to-GDP: 101%
AU Federal Budget: 12 May 2026
US 30Y UST: briefly 5.00% (term premium re-pricing)
US Q2 borrowing: +$79bn above February estimate
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price the fiscal differential as structural but not actionable tactically. No mispricing at this horizon.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At strategic horizons, the fiscal differential becomes more material as AUD-positive. The US twin deficit trajectory, rising debt-service costs ($1trn in FY2026), and weak Treasury auctions (late-March 2Y BTC 2.44 vs avg 2.62) create structural term-premium pressure that weighs on USD. Australia's fiscal headroom and AAA rating provide structural AUD support in any global fiscal-stress scenario. If sovereign credit quality becomes a market theme through H2 2026 (triggered by further weak US auctions or rating outlook changes), AUD would benefit from flight-to-quality flows within the G10.
key_bilateral_metrics: US net interest: 17% of revenue (approaching 20% historical threshold)
US twin deficit: -8.8% of GDP (worst since 1985)
AU 2024-25 deficit: AUD 10bn (better than forecast)
US 30Y term premium re-pricing: ongoing
Probability of US rating outlook change 6M: ~10%
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price gradual US fiscal deterioration. The unpriced tail is a discrete fiscal-stress event (auction failure, rating action) that would sharply benefit AUD vs USD.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Neutral (fiscal rarely moves in 4 weeks); strategic Bullish (fiscal differential becomes material over 6 months through the term-premium channel).

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/USD is in a bullishly stacked MA configuration (spot 0.7247 > 50d 0.7071 > 100d 0.7061 > 200d 0.6841) that supports trend-following CTA longs. However, the pair has reached key resistance at the May 2022 swing area (0.7265–0.7283) and is showing distribution characteristics after the 6 May spike to 0.7280 was immediately sold. The AUDUSD broke sharply higher, extending above the April and May swing highs between 0.7221 and 0.7227, but the rally carried the pair to a session high of 0.72769, pushing into a key swing area resistance zone from May 2022 between 0.72656 and 0.7283, where sellers leaned against that historical resistance area. IV at 8–9% (35–45th percentile) means options are cheap relative to positioning extremes, creating asymmetric downside risk not priced in vol markets. The technical picture is constructive but extended — upside breakout above 0.7283 would confirm trend continuation, while failure and reversal below 0.7193 would signal distribution.
key_bilateral_metrics: 52-week range percentile: 98th (extreme upper)
Resistance: 0.7265–0.7283 (May 2022 swing), 0.7300 (psychological), 0.7335–0.7350
Support: 0.7193–0.7200 (swing), 0.7080 (bullish invalidation), 0.7000 (psychological)
1M ATM IV: ~8.5% (estimate); IV percentile: ~35–45th
tactical.what_is_priced: Vol markets price modest event premium; IV at the 35–45th percentile is cheap relative to positioning extremes. Downside puts offer asymmetric payoff if positioning unwinds.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic technical picture is more balanced. The 12-month rally of +12.6% is mature but the bullish MA stack remains intact — no regime change signalled until the 200d MA (0.6841) breaks. The 200d MA at 0.6841 is the bull/bear regime line for the rest of 2026. A test of this level has elevated probability (~30%) over 6 months given the catalyst calendar and positioning extremes, but is not the base case. Structurally, AUD/USD has more room to the upside over 6 months because USD weakness is the dominant strategic theme, even if the near-term rate is extended.
key_bilateral_metrics: 12-month rally: +12.6% (mature but not extreme for AUD cycles)
200d SMA: 0.6841 (regime line)
Probability of 200d test (6M): ~30%
Strategic resistance: 0.7300, 0.7400 (2017 trend resistance)
Strategic support: 0.7000, 0.6841
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price orderly trend continuation. The unpriced element is the tail risk of a positioning-driven correction that tests lower support levels before the structural uptrend resumes.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Neutral, reflecting the tension between constructive trend structure and extended positioning at resistance.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: AUD/USD IV at 8–9% (35–45th percentile) is cheap relative to positioning extremes and the binary catalyst cluster. The vol regime is low and compressing, meaning options are attractively priced for both directional bets and hedges.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Given cheap vol and near-resistance positioning, the optimal expression of a bullish AUD/USD view is to buy dips toward the 0.7080–0.7100 zone rather than chase the current level near 0.7247. For downside hedges or bearish positioning plays on the crowded positioning story, 1M 0.7000 puts or 0.7000–0.6850 put spreads offer asymmetric payoff given cheap IV. Spot expression remains efficient for moderate-conviction long entries on pullbacks.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The binary data cluster of 12–15 May (US CPI, AU Budget, AU WPI, AU Labour Force) creates event vol that argues for options expression if directional conviction is high. Holding through the FOMC June 17 (Warsh's first) is a meaningful event risk — gamma hedging appropriate around that date.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: AUD/USD is one of the top 5 most liquid G10 FX pairs (typical daily volume $400–500bn). Execution is efficient at all standard sizes. Carry-to-vol is materially better in AUD/JPY (0.38) and AUD/NZD (0.32) than AUD/USD (0.09) — consider expressing the AUD bullish view through these crosses for better risk-adjusted carry.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: -0.03 tactical / -0.02 strategic: modest reduction because USD liquidity is benign (no stress premium to allocate to); offset used to fund positioning weight increase

tactical.bilateral_analysis: USD funding conditions are abundant and orderly, which pushes USD to the bottom of the dollar smile — a regime that is structurally AUD/USD-positive. Fed balance sheet at ~$6.5trn (QT concluded December 2025), bank reserves at ~$3.4trn (~13% of GDP, well above minimum), SOFR pinned near IORB, and zero Fed FX swap-line draws since February all confirm no USD funding stress. AUD/USD cross-currency basis is estimated at -5 to -10bp (normal range, no stress). For the pair specifically, abundant USD liquidity means no funding-driven demand for USD, allowing the structural bearish-USD thesis to operate unimpeded. The AUD/USD forward curve reflects this: negative forward points (AUD carries over USD) generate positive carry for long AUD/USD spot positions.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed balance sheet: ~$6.5trn (QT ended Dec 2025)
Bank reserves: ~$3.4trn (~13% GDP, ample)
AUD/USD 3M XCCY basis: estimated -5 to -10bp (no stress)
Fed FX swap draws: ZERO since Feb 28
RRP usage: ~$632bn (substantial buffer)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price abundant funding — no stress premium. The dollar-smile bottom regime supports AUD/USD at current levels.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the USD funding regime continues to support AUD/USD. Warsh's expected dovish shift implies more accommodative Fed liquidity conditions through H2 2026. The dollar-smile framework places USD at the bottom (weak in goldilocks) with the base case being sustained goldilocks through the strategic window. The only stress scenario would be a fiscal-driven auction failure or sudden Iran re-escalation triggering dollar-smile left-side activation — both tail risks (15–25% combined probability). Year-end (December 2026) USD funding squeeze is the only seasonal stress point within the strategic window. For Australian banks, offshore USD funding needs are well-managed with no stress signals; the RBA-Fed swap line remains in place.
key_bilateral_metrics: Dollar smile position: USD at bottom (goldilocks, risk-on)
Warsh policy bias: more dovish than expected
Year-end USD squeeze probability: modest (December)
AU bank USD funding: stable, no stress
Strategic transition risk: 15–25% combined tail probability
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price benign USD funding. Strategic edge is in the magnitude of Warsh-driven dovishness — more cuts than priced would keep USD at the dollar-smile bottom for longer.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Bullish at same confidence (58), reflecting consistent dollar-smile-bottom regime across both windows.

adjustments.reason: Bilateral double-crowding at extremes (AUD longs +93k at 85–90th percentile AND USD shorts at decade extreme) warrants higher weight than default; amplified unwind risk specific to this pair
Modest reduction because USD liquidity is benign with no stress premium; freed weight allocated to positioning factor. Reduction offset maintains sum of 1.00.
Same bilateral double-crowding rationale applies strategically; +0.02 adjustment
-0.02 strategic reduction; benign USD funding regime warrants lower weight at strategic horizon

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score of +0.344 maps to Mildly Bullish with moderate conviction (3/5). The score is positive but below the +0.50 threshold for high conviction, primarily because the positioning factor (-0.094) provides a significant drag on an otherwise bullish fundamental setup.
tactical.rationale: The tactical horizon is dominated by the policy-rates factor (+0.173 weighted), where the RBA-Fed divergence at +73bp with diverging paths creates the strongest bilateral carry signal in G10. Inflation/growth dynamics (+0.117) reinforce this as Australia's decisive tightening contrasts with US stagflation paralysis. Risk appetite (+0.060) and USD liquidity (+0.046) add further bullish contributions through the dollar-smile-bottom mechanism. These four factors together contribute +0.396 to the weighted score. The critical offset is flows & positioning (-0.094), where bilateral double-crowding at extreme levels creates the highest single-factor bearish signal in the analysis. The dossier interaction is reinforcing for AUD/USD: the AUD dossier's mild bullish tilt (+0.253) combines with the USD dossier's bearish tilt (-0.134) to produce an amplified pair-level bullish signal. The bilateral-specific factor that modifies standalone views is the positioning double-crowding — neither dossier alone captures the amplified unwind risk when both legs are at extremes simultaneously. What is priced vs expected: current spot at 0.7247 appears to fully price the peak-carry premium but not the likely further widening of the AU-US rate gap under Warsh. Path of least resistance: consolidation in the 0.7100–0.7280 range with bias toward the upper end if WPI prints firm and US CPI is soft. Key catalysts within the horizon: 12 May CPI (USD binary), 12 May Federal Budget (AUD), 14 May WPI (AUD binary), 15 May Labour Force (AUD binary). Conviction at 3/5 rather than higher because the positioning risk is genuine and the catalysts could resolve either way.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/USD ranks as the 3rd most attractive G10 long behind EUR/USD (strongest structural USD-bearish expression) and USD/JPY short (MOF intervention support + BoJ hiking). Among AUD crosses, AUD/JPY offers better carry-to-vol (0.38 vs 0.09) and AUD/NZD offers a cleaner rate-differential play (210bp vs 73bp). AUD/USD is the broadest expression of both AUD strength and USD weakness simultaneously but carries the highest positioning risk.

conditions: Hormuz remains contested with no definitive ceasefire
RBA pauses June with hawkish hold as expected
WPI prints 3.4–3.6% (in-line with consensus)
US CPI in-line at 3.3%
base_case.path: Consolidation range 0.7150–0.7280 as markets digest the May SMP and await the 12–15 May data cluster; mild pullback toward 0.7200 as buy-the-rumour sell-the-news dynamics play out post-RBA hike

conditions: WPI surprises to 3.6% or above, extending RBA hike expectations
US CPI prints cool at or below 3.1%
Definitive Iran deal signed removing safe-haven USD bid
Hormuz reopens definitively with Brent below $90 triggering AUD long unwind
Hot US CPI at 3.5% or above triggers short-USD positioning squeeze
Soft April AU employment with unemployment rising to 4.5%

tactical.path_expectation: The expected pattern is a two-phase tactical structure. Phase 1 (weeks 1-2): Range-bound consolidation 0.7150–0.7280 as markets digest the May SMP and await the 12–15 May data cluster. The 0.7193–0.7200 swing support and 0.7265–0.7283 resistance define the immediate range. Phase 2 (weeks 3-4): Directional break determined by the data cluster — base case of mild pullback toward 0.7200 as buy-the-rumour, sell-the-news dynamics play out post-RBA hike. Volatility expectation is moderate (1M IV ~8.5%) with a spike potential around the mid-May data cluster. Pullback levels: 0.7193 first, 0.7100 second, 0.7000 third. The pair is unlikely to break sustainably above 0.7283 without a significant positive catalyst surprise, given the resistance overhead and crowded positioning dynamics.

increase_conditions: Increase to 4 if: WPI 3.6% AND US CPI at or below 3.1% AND AUD/USD breaks 0.7283 sustained on daily close
Increase to 5 if: All above PLUS CFTC AUD longs drop below +70k (positioning risk removed) AND Brent stable around $100
decrease_conditions: Decrease to 2 if: Hormuz reopens with Brent sustainably below $90 OR soft AU labour force with unemployment at 4.5%
Decrease to 1 if: AUD/USD breaks below 0.7000 on positioning unwind AND hot US CPI triggers Fed hawkish repricing

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: Mildly negative — upside case +103 pips vs downside case -197 pips
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The distribution is negatively skewed at current entry levels because the pair sits at the 98th percentile of its 52-week range. Better risk/reward on pullbacks toward 0.7100–0.7150 where the upside/downside is more balanced.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score +0.276 maps to Mildly Bullish with moderate conviction (3/5). The strategic score is lower than tactical (+0.276 vs +0.344) reflecting reduced inflation/growth and risk-appetite contributions, partially offset by the addition of fiscal/sovereign support.
strategic.rationale: The strategic picture is dominated by the durable RBA-Fed rate-path divergence (+0.144) and the bilateral credibility gap (+0.074), supplemented by US fiscal deterioration supporting AUD (+0.046) and dollar-smile-bottom dynamics (+0.058). These four bullish contributors total +0.322, partially offset by positioning headwinds (-0.046). The key strategic dynamic is that AUD/USD benefits from both legs: AUD is supported by the highest G10 policy rate and terms-of-trade premium, while USD weakens from structural twin-deficit drag, Fed institutional uncertainty, and rate convergence. The AUD dossier's strategic view is neutral on AUD/USD (suggesting fade toward 0.69–0.70), while the USD dossier is bearish USD (targeting DXY 92–95). The bilateral synthesis resolves this by recognising that AUD/USD may pull back to 0.70–0.71 in Q3 as the RBA approaches terminal, but then rally into year-end as Fed easing deepens. The net path over 6 months is modestly higher — target is 0.73–0.74 by Q4 2026. The conviction driver is the structural USD weakness (9 of 10 factors bearish in the USD dossier) combined with durable RBA rate advantage. Conviction is moderate (3/5) rather than high because the wide distribution of outcomes (Hormuz resolution path, Q2 CPI, China) creates genuine two-way risk at the pair level. The strategic path features three distinct phases: consolidation (May–June), pullback (July–August), and resumption (September–November), meaning the 6-month view is not a linear grind higher but rather a consolidation-dip-rally pattern that rewards patient positioning.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/USD ranks 4th among G10 longs strategically, behind EUR/USD (1st, cleanest structural USD-bearish), USD/JPY short (2nd, BoJ hiking + intervention support), and USD/CHF short (3rd, structural safe-haven). Among AUD crosses: AUD/JPY long remains the highest-conviction AUD expression; AUD/NZD long offers the cleanest rate-differential play. AUD/USD is broader but riskier due to the tension between AUD positioning extremes and USD structural weakness, earning a lower ranking than pairs with cleaner risk/reward profiles.

conditions: RBA pauses through August then resumes hawkish rhetoric if Q2 CPI sticky above 3.5%
Fed delivers 50–75bp cuts under Warsh through H2 2026
Hormuz partially normalises with Brent reverting to $80–90
China growth holds near 5% supporting iron ore at $90–100
base_case.path: Phase 1 (May–June) consolidation 0.71–0.73; Phase 2 (July–August) pullback to 0.70–0.71 as RBA approaches terminal and August seasonality weighs; Phase 3 (September–November) rally to 0.73–0.74 as Fed easing deepens and AU-US differential widens. Pattern is consolidation-dip-rally, not linear.

conditions: Continued Hormuz disruption with Brent remaining above $100
RBA delivers another hike to 4.60% and Fed cuts 100bp+
China stimulus surprise driving iron ore above $115
Hormuz fully reopens with Brent falling sustainably below $80
China property deterioration with iron ore below $85
RBA delivers dovish pivot and US recession drives flight to USD

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path features three phases over 6 months. Phase 1 (May–June): Consolidation in the 0.71–0.73 range, with the 12–15 May data cluster and 16–17 June RBA/FOMC meetings setting the directional tone for the medium term. Phase 2 (July–August): Potential pullback to 0.70–0.71 as the RBA approaches terminal, Q2 CPI resolves (late July), and August seasonality weighs on risk-sensitive currencies including AUD. This is the key buying opportunity for strategic longs. Phase 3 (September–November): Rally to 0.73–0.74 as Fed easing deepens, USD structural weakness accelerates into midterms, and the AU-US rate differential widens to +100–135bp. The pattern shape is consolidation-dip-rally rather than a linear grind higher, and the July–August dip should be used to add to long positions rather than exit.

increase_conditions: Increase to 4 if: Fed delivers 50bp+ cuts by September AND Q2 AU trimmed mean 3.5% confirming RBA holds high AND AUD/USD holds above 0.7100 through the pullback phase
Increase to 5 if: All above PLUS CFTC positioning normalises below +60k removing contrarian headwind AND China stimulus surprise drives iron ore above $115
decrease_conditions: Decrease to 2 if: Hormuz fully reopens with Brent sustainably below $85 OR China Q2 GDP misses at below 4.5%
Decrease to 1 if: AUD/USD breaks below 0.6841 (200d SMA) on weekly close — regime change signal

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly positive — base case and upside case combined outweigh downside case on probability-weighted basis, supporting a strategic long bias with the best entry on pullbacks toward 0.7100–0.7150.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Bank of America: 0.74 year-end 2026. Bloomberg consensus: 0.71 year-end. Major bank range: 0.69–0.73. NAB: 0.7200–0.7300. AMP: 0.70–0.75 coming months. Sources: [1], [5], [8], [9].
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Mildly Bullish; base case AUD/USD consolidates 0.7150–0.7280, ending near 0.7220 over 4 weeks
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Mildly Bullish; target 0.73–0.74 by Q4 2026, at the upper end of the consensus range
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: +200 to +300 pips above Bloomberg consensus year-end forecast of 0.71
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Our strategic view of 0.73–0.74 by Q4 2026 sits at the upper end of the bank consensus range (0.69–0.74), broadly aligned with Bank of America's bullish call. We differ from the Bloomberg consensus of 0.71 by expecting the RBA rate advantage to persist longer (Q2 trimmed mean sticky above 3.5% pushing the first RBA cut into late 2027) while Fed easing runs deeper than consensus (75bp+ vs 25–50bp priced). The key bilateral factor we weight differently is the US institutional credibility erosion under Warsh — most banks treat the transition as neutral, while we view it as USD-negative due to the dovish Warsh surprise potential and the institutional fracture signal from the 8-4 FOMC dissent. This credibility differential is a persistent AUD/USD tailwind that most bank models underweight.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Strategic long AUD/USD from 0.7200 (entered on pullback) to 0.7400 = +200 pips / +2.8%
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: AUD/USD settles at 0.71 (consensus) — opportunity cost of 100 pips from 0.7200 entry but no structural loss if stop managed above 0.6841

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic stances are aligned (both Mildly Bullish at conviction 3/5), supporting consistent positioning across time horizons. The lower strategic score (+0.276 vs +0.344) reflects the narrowing of some bullish factors (inflation/growth convergence, risk appetite uncertainty) offset by the emergence of fiscal support. No stance reversal is expected within the 6-month window.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Long AUD/USD on pullbacks toward 0.7100–0.7150 with a 6-month hold targeting 0.7350–0.7400; use the mid-year pullback (July–August) to add to the position rather than fighting it. Tactical chop in the 0.71–0.73 range is the expected near-term path.

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~0.7243 (-4 pts); 0.5% annualised AUD depreciation in forwards
forward_vs_spot.expected_rate_3m: 0.7150 (below forward by 85 pips — short-AUD edge on pullback)
forward_vs_spot.expected_rate_6m: 0.7350 (above forward by 135 pips — meaningful long-AUD edge)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: -85 pips vs forward implied 0.7235 (pullback expected to overshoot forward)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +135 pips vs forward implied 0.7215 (meaningful positive edge; cleanest strategic expression)

catalysts.rationale: Budget expected to feature modest fiscal expansion with productivity package and energy bill rebates. Tight fiscal would marginally support AUD via lower bond supply; expansionary stance marginally negative via higher issuance. Typical budget AUD impact is 5–15 pips and rarely sustained beyond the session. Bilateral transmission: affects AUD leg only through bond supply and sovereign confidence channels; USD unaffected.

Highest-impact tactical catalyst for AUD/USD. A hot print at or above +0.4% MoM would strengthen USD via hawkish Fed repricing and trigger short-USD positioning squeeze, pushing AUD/USD lower by 100–150 pips as both legs move against AUD. A cool print at or below +0.2% MoM would weaken USD, supporting AUD/USD higher by 50–100 pips. Consensus +0.3%/3.4% YoY. Bilateral transmission: USD leg moves sharply; AUD leg unaffected directly but positioning unwind affects both simultaneously.

WPI above 3.6% YoY would extend RBA hawkish expectations and widen the AU-US rate differential, directly benefiting AUD/USD via the primary rates channel. Below 3.3% would dampen the wage-inflation link and reduce the RBA hawkish premium. A firm WPI combined with a soft US CPI (12 May) within the same week would create a powerful bilateral bullish signal amplifying both legs. Consensus 3.3–3.5%; upside surprise above 3.6% not priced.

Employment growth consistent with trend (+15k, unemployment at or below 4.3%) supports RBA hawkish stance and extends AUD carry premium. A soft print with unemployment rising to 4.5% would trigger positioning unwind and AUD/USD selloff of potentially 2–3% given the crowded long-AUD positioning. The bilateral asymmetry is important: upside employment surprise adds modestly to a pair already near highs, while downside surprise triggers disproportionate liquidation given stretched longs.

Warsh viewed as more dovish than his hawk reputation suggests; first public commentary as incoming chair will set reaction-function expectations for markets. A dovish surprise (focus on growth risks over inflation) would weaken USD and widen the bilateral rate differential expectation, supporting AUD/USD. A hawkish surprise would narrow the bilateral credibility gap and partially unwind the bearish-USD thesis. Bilateral transmission: USD leg moves based on Warsh's framing; AUD leg largely unaffected.

Westpac nowcasting Q4 at +1.1% QoQ. A print at or above this level justifies RBA hawkish stance and supports AUD through the growth channel. A print below +0.5% QoQ signals growth fatigue inconsistent with the hawkish rates thesis, which could trigger AUD downside. Bilateral transmission: affects AUD leg through RBA reaction-function expectations; USD unaffected.

Approximately 80% probability of hold. A pause confirmation removes the marginal hawkish bid but does not change the structural thesis as peak-rate proximity is already priced. A surprise hike (approximately 20% probability) would be sharply AUD-positive through the bilateral rate channel, potentially driving AUD/USD to 0.7350+. Bilateral transmission: affects AUD leg directly; any hawkish surprise amplifies the AU-US differential.

First FOMC meeting under Warsh with new Summary of Economic Projections and dot plot. A dovish dot revision (cutting the median 2026 dot from current implied 3.625% toward 3.00%) would crystallise the USD structural weakness thesis and widen the AU-US rate differential materially, supporting AUD/USD toward 0.7300+. A hawkish hold with unchanged dots would provide USD support and modest AUD/USD headwinds. Bilateral transmission: USD leg moves directly on dot plot; AUD leg benefits from widening implied differential.

The RBA SMP forecasts Q2 trimmed mean at 3.8% (peak). A print above this level confirms extended hawkish RBA stance and is the single most powerful AUD/USD bullish catalyst in the strategic window, potentially driving the pair to 0.7400+. A print below 3.3% triggers AUD cut expectations and would likely drive AUD/USD below 0.7100 as the rate differential thesis collapses. This is the binary pivot point for the strategic view. Bilateral transmission: affects both legs — AUD directly via RBA path, USD indirectly via global reflation narrative.

catalysts.event: Jackson Hole Symposium (Warsh's first framework speech)
catalysts.rationale: Warsh's first major framework speech at Jackson Hole will set the policy framing for the remainder of 2026. A dovish framework (emphasising growth downside risks, flagging multiple cuts) would be a significant USD-negative event, potentially driving DXY below 97 and AUD/USD above 0.7350. A hawkish surprise would narrow the bilateral credibility gap and support USD recovery. Bilateral transmission: USD leg moves directly on framework signals; AUD leg unaffected independently.

invalidators.condition: AUD/USD breaks below 0.6841 (200d SMA) on a weekly close basis
invalidators.mechanism: This regime-line break would trigger systematic CTA selling across quant models, structural positioning unwind from stretched long-AUD positioning, and likely 5–7% further downside momentum. The 200d MA break would signal that the 22% post-April 2025 rally has fully unwound, removing the primary technical support for the bullish thesis.

invalidators.condition: Brent crude returns below $80/bbl on full Hormuz reopening sustained for 5+ trading days
invalidators.mechanism: The commodity/inflation premium driving both RBA hikes and AUD outperformance would evaporate: lower Brent reduces RBA hawkish justification (lower future CPI), removes AUD terms-of-trade premium via LNG channel, and simultaneously re-prices the Iran-risk premium that supported the pair above 0.72. The positioning unwind (from +93k AUD longs) would amplify the fundamental move.

invalidators.condition: CFTC combined AUD speculative and asset manager net longs exceed +110k contracts
invalidators.mechanism: Positioning would reach extreme fragility levels with unwind potential of 5–8% in AUD/USD. At +110k, any negative catalyst would trigger cascading stops across both the AUD long and USD short legs simultaneously, generating an outsized pair selloff with no natural buyers to absorb the flow. This positioning extreme would upgrade the contrarian signal from bearish-tilt to high-conviction bearish.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: CFTC weekly TFF report — AUD combined net position

invalidators.condition: Q2 AU trimmed mean CPI prints below 3.0% YoY
invalidators.mechanism: The RBA hawkish justification would be removed and cut expectations would be pulled forward from Q1 2027 to Q4 2026, erasing the rate-differential advantage that is the primary AUD/USD bullish driver. The AU-US policy gap would compress from +73bp toward +25bp or less, removing the primary factor that scores +0.173 in the tactical weighted score.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: ABS Q2 CPI release (estimated late July 2026)

invalidators.condition: US CPI accelerates above 4.0% YoY forcing the Fed to signal hikes rather than cuts
invalidators.mechanism: AU-US rate differential compression would remove the primary AUD/USD bullish driver as the Fed reverses toward tightening. The USD structural weakness thesis (dollar-smile bottom) would flip to dollar-smile top, generating broad USD strength across all G10 pairs including AUD/USD. The current bearish positioning on USD would unwind sharply, amplifying the pair's downside.

invalidators.condition: China Q2 GDP misses consensus at below 4.5% YoY with iron ore falling below $85/t sustained
invalidators.mechanism: A Chinese growth shock would simultaneously remove the commodity export demand supporting AUD terms of trade and trigger broad emerging-market risk-off flows that would lift USD as a safe haven. Australia's 30–35% export dependence on China means a Chinese growth surprise has outsized AUD impact relative to any other G10 currency, making this the clearest AUD-specific bearish catalyst.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: China National Bureau of Statistics quarterly GDP release and daily iron ore 62% Fe CFR China price

signposts.confirms_if: Widens above +80bp on combination of dovish Fed and RBA hawkish hold
signposts.invalidates_if: Narrows below +30bp on hawkish Fed shift or RBA dovish pivot
signposts.bilateral_importance: Primary rate-channel driver of AUD/USD; directly captures the bilateral policy divergence that accounts for the highest weighted factor contribution (+0.173 tactical). This spread is the real-time market pricing of the AU-US rate differential that underpins the entire bullish thesis.

signposts.indicator: Iron Ore 62% Fe CFR China Spot Price
signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above $110/t for 5+ consecutive trading days, signalling Chinese demand resilience
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below $90/t sustained over 5+ days, signalling Chinese demand deterioration
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD terms-of-trade primary driver with pure AUD sensitivity and no offsetting USD effect — unlike oil which affects both legs. AUD/USD has historically shown 60–70% correlation with iron ore during commodity-driven cycles. A $20 move in iron ore typically corresponds to approximately 1.5–2% AUD/USD move in the same direction.

signposts.confirms_if: Settles and holds in the $90–110 range, supporting AUD commodity premium without triggering extreme risk-off USD safe-haven bid
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below $80 sustained (removes AUD commodity premium via LNG channel) OR spikes above $130 (triggers extreme risk-off USD bid that overpowers AUD commodity benefit)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Affects BOTH pair legs asymmetrically: AUD benefits via LNG export revenues and RBA inflation hawkishness extension, while USD benefits via petrodollar and safe-haven flows. The net impact on AUD/USD depends on the speed of move — gradual changes are mildly AUD-positive at $90–110, but sharp moves in either direction are USD-positive via safe-haven or positioning dynamics.

signposts.indicator: CFTC AUD Combined Net Long Positioning (Speculative + Asset Manager)
signposts.confirms_if: Normalises toward +50–60k contracts, reducing the positioning headwind without signalling a sentiment reversal that would undermine the structural bullish thesis
signposts.invalidates_if: Builds above +110k (fragility extreme, high unwind risk) OR collapses below +30k rapidly in under 4 weeks (panic unwind indicating structural narrative breakdown)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The most direct measure of crowding risk specific to the AUD leg of this pair. At +93k the pair is at the threshold of historically dangerous positioning levels. This is the primary counter-weight to the fundamentally bullish factor scores — a reduction in longs without a narrative collapse would actually improve the pair's risk/reward.
signposts.frequency: Weekly (CFTC TFF report, published Friday for previous Tuesday)

signposts.indicator: RBA Cash Rate Pricing — OIS-Implied September 2026 Meeting
signposts.current_level: ~68% probability of additional 25bp hike to 4.60% by August/September 2026
signposts.confirms_if: Probability rises above 80% on sticky Q2 CPI or firm WPI prints, extending the hawkish premium
signposts.invalidates_if: Probability falls below 30%, implying markets are pricing RBA cut cycle start, which would eliminate the primary rate differential driving AUD/USD upside
signposts.bilateral_importance: Directly drives the bilateral rate differential that is the primary AUD/USD factor (tactical weight 0.24, highest of all 10 factors). This indicator provides daily real-time repricing of the bilateral carry advantage — more responsive than the CFTC positioning report and faster than macroeconomic releases.

signposts.confirms_if: Breaks below 97.00 and sustains, confirming broad USD weakness that benefits AUD/USD through the USD leg
signposts.invalidates_if: Rallies above 100.50, indicating safe-haven bid or hawkish Fed repricing that would compress AUD/USD through the quote leg
signposts.bilateral_importance: Proxy for the broad USD dynamics that affect AUD/USD through the quote leg. DXY at the 97–100 level represents the key zone where USD structural weakness (justified by fiscal, institutional, and rate-path factors) either accelerates or reverses. AUD/USD has historically traded inversely with DXY at approximately -0.75 correlation over rolling 60-day windows.

assumptions: Price data (Twelve Data daily OHLC) is used as source of truth for spot (0.7247), performance figures, moving averages (50d 0.7071, 100d 0.7061, 200d 0.6841), 52-week range (0.5915–0.7280), and trend regime assessment. Web search was used for support/resistance levels, bank forecasts, and bilateral flow dynamics.

Forward points estimated from the approximately 73bp rate differential; precise OTC forward quotes were not retrieved from public sources. Carry-to-vol ratios calculated using estimated 1M ATM IV of approximately 8.5% which represents a midpoint of the 8–9% range.

Bank consensus forecasts compiled from pre-May SMP reports; some banks may have revised post the 5 May RBA hike and March trade data publication. Bloomberg consensus of 0.71 year-end and BofA's 0.74 target represent the range boundaries used for divergence analysis.

The March 2026 trade deficit (AUD -1.84bn, first since December 2017) is a new development not fully captured in either standalone dossier. This weakens the AUD external-balance case relative to the AUD dossier's bullish terms-of-trade assessment and is treated as a tactical negative for Factor 5.

Weight adjustments: positioning weight boosted by +0.03 tactical and +0.02 strategic due to bilateral double-crowding at extreme levels in both AUD longs and USD shorts simultaneously; USD liquidity weight reduced by -0.03 tactical and -0.02 strategic to maintain sum of 1.00. These adjustments reflect the bilateral-specific amplification of positioning risk not captured by standalone dossiers.

Conviction scoring convention: Bullish = +1, Neutral = 0, Bearish = -1, multiplied by confidence/100 to produce confidence-adjusted score, then multiplied by applied weight. Net weighted scores: +0.20 to +0.50 = Moderate conviction (3/5); below +0.20 = Low conviction (2/5); above +0.50 = High conviction (4–5/5).

IV percentile estimates (35–45th) are derived from comparison to historical vol levels described in source materials; precise OTC vol surface data was not available from public sources. RV estimates derived from recent daily range analysis of price data.

The Hormuz scenario probabilities (25–30% reopening within 4 weeks, 60% lasting ceasefire within 6 months) are analytical estimates based on available geopolitical reporting and should be treated as directional rather than precise probability estimates.

CFTC positioning percentile (85–90th of 5-year rolling window) is an estimate based on the absolute contract count (+92,952) compared to reported historical highs; the precise percentile rank was not confirmed from a direct CFTC database query.
